name: brawn

metadata.name: brawn
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU General Public License v3 or later (GPLv3+)
metadata.summary: A tool for handling repetitive insertions into sequence alignments

documentation:
# brawn
A python port of [MUSCLE](https://drive5.com/muscle/downloads_v3.htm)'s
profile-profile mode for aligning sequences.

Brawn was specifically designed with repetitive insertions of query sequences
into reference alignments in mind.

Brawn takes advantage of that repetitive nature by using cache files to remove
the calculations that would need to be repeated over and over for the reference
alignments. This counters the speed loss of moving from C to python. Initially
building these cache files will take about 10 times longer than an equivalent
single run.

While results are often identical to those from MUSCLE, there will occasionally
be some minor variations. If an alignment would be very poor in the best case,
this variation will be more pronounced.

Only protein sequences are explicitly supported at the moment, full DNA/RNA
support may follow later.

## Installation
### from PyPI
```
pip install brawn
```
### from source
```
pip install .
```
### for development
```
pip install .[testing]
```

## Use
For speed, using a cache file is extremely important. They can be built and used
both programatically and the command line. They are also optional, if required.

### Basic profile-profile mode

To load an alignment from a FASTA file, use:
```
from brawn import Alignment

with open("some.fasta") as handle:
    alignment = Alignment.from_file(handle)
```

Or, to build from an existing dictionary (even if it's just one element), use:
```
name_to_sequence = {
    "A": "GT-DVG",
    "B": "GTK-VG",
}
alignment = Alignment(name_to_sequence)
```

Or, to load cached calculations directly (this is the preferable option for large alignments):
```
with open("reference.cache") as handle:
    alignment = Alignment.from_cache_file(handle)
```

As a sidenote, to build a cache for the reference alignment:
```
with open("reference.cache", "w") as handle:
    alignment.to_cache_file(handle)
```
This will add any needed computational steps prior to writing.

Once two alignments have been created/loaded, they can be combined with:
```
from brawn import combine_alignments

result = combine_alignments(alignment1, alignment2)
```

And the resulting alignment can be output to file:
```
with open("output.fasta", "w") as handle:
    result.to_file(handle=handle)

# if handle is not supplied, the output will be sent to sys.stdout
results.to_file()
```

### Simpler insertion of a sequence into an alignment

For the use case that brawn was designed for, only a single sequence is being
inserted into a reference alignment at a time. This is usually paired with
fetching a single reference sequence, and finding the matching sites.

```
# note: this still aligns with the full reference alignment
# only the reference sequence mentioned by name will be built
aligned_query, aligned_reference = get_aligned_pair(query_sequence, reference_name,
                                                    reference_alignment)

```

Or, if you prefer to have the full set of newly aligned reference sequences as a dictionary:
```
aligned_query, aligned_refs_by_name = insert_into_alignment(query_sequence, alignment)
```

### From the command line
Brawn can function as a drop-in replacement for MUSCLE's `-profile` mode, complete
command line argument conversion.

To take advantage of cached alignment calculations, first build a cache of your
alignment, with:
```
brawn input.fasta --build-cache desired_cache_path
```

From that point, you can use cached files and plain FASTA files interchangeably.
E.g.
```
brawn query.fasta --reference-alignment some_fasta_file
```
and
```
brawn query.fasta --reference-alignment some_cache_file
```
will both work as expected.

For the command line, the resulting FASTA output will to be stdout.

modules.name: brawn.positions
documentation:
Classes and functions for handling data and calculations of particular
positions/columns within alignments

documentation: Scores two positions from two different alignments

functions.name: brawn.positions.set_terminal_gaps
documentation: (Re)Sets the gap scores for the start/end positions of the given list

documentation: Convert the position a JSON-friendly representation

documentation: Reconstructs a position from a JSON-friendly representation
Data for a single character position within an alignment

documentation: Contains various alphabet handling code

functions.name: brawn.alphabet.is_gap_char
documentation: Returns True if the given character is a gap character

functions.name: brawn.alphabet.is_wildcard
documentation:
Returns True if the given character is a wildcard/ambiguity character
in the given alphabet

functions.name: brawn.alphabet.is_residue
documentation: Returns True if the given character is a residue in the given alphabet

classes.name: brawn.alphabet.Alphabet

methods.name: brawn.alphabet.Alphabet.from_string
documentation: Selects an alphabet from various string options

methods.name: brawn.alphabet.Alphabet.get_size
documentation: Returns the number of residues in the given alphabet
Possible sequence alphabets

modules.name: brawn.constants
documentation: A collection of constants for use throughout brawn

modules.name: brawn.distance
documentation: Distance calculation methods

functions.name: brawn.distance.calculate_distance
documentation:
Calculates a distance from the given similarity, via different methods
depending on where in the range 0-1 the similarity falls.
straight from MUSCLE

modules.name: brawn
documentation: Exposing the parts of brawn most likely to be used

modules.name: brawn.tree
documentation: Tree related code

functions.name: brawn.tree._normalise
documentation:
Constructs a normalised list of values from the input,
with the sum of the result being 1

classes.name: brawn.tree.Tree

methods.name: brawn.tree.Tree.get_parent
documentation: Returns the parent of the given node index

methods.name: brawn.tree.Tree.get_left
documentation: Returns the left child of the given node index

methods.name: brawn.tree.Tree.get_right
documentation: Returns the right child of the given node index

methods.name: brawn.tree.Tree.is_leaf
documentation: Returns True if it's a leaf

methods.name: brawn.tree.Tree.get_edge_length
documentation: Returns the length between two neighbouring nodes

methods.name: brawn.tree.Tree.node_child_counts
documentation:
The counts of leaves under a nodes in the tree (including the node itself)

Returns:
    a list of counts, each one the number of leaves under the relevant node

methods.name: brawn.tree.Tree.get_weights
documentation:
Calculates sequence weights from a tree of the sequences

Arguments:
    tree: the tree of sequences

Returns:
    a normalised list of weights, summing to 1
A binary tree for sequences within an alignment

modules.name: brawn.path
documentation: A collection of useful data types for paths through a matrix

classes.name: brawn.path.Modification
documentation: A modification type for a sequence, based on the path direction

classes.name: brawn.path.Edge

methods.name: brawn.path.Edge.copy
documentation: Creates a copy of the instance

An collection of data of an edge in a path, with:
type: the type of edge
query_length: the number of steps to reach this in the query
reference_length: the number of steps to reach this in the reference

classes.name: brawn.path.Path
documentation: A path of edges through a matrix

modules.name: brawn.align
documentation: Functions for running a global alignment of two existing alignments

functions.name: brawn.align.global_align
documentation:
Builds and returns a Path for the two given alignments being merged
into a single alignment

functions.name: brawn.align.get_modification
documentation: Gets the matching modification instance from an

functions.name: brawn.align.build_path
documentation:
Builds a path through the matrix, beginning at the most distant point

Arguments:
    traceback: the traceback matrix
    query_length: the number of columns in the query
    reference_length: the number of columns in the reference
    last_edge: the edge type to use for that last position

Returns:
    a Path, with edges ordered from the beginning of the sequences
    to the end

modules.name: brawn.alignment
documentation:
A collection of classes and functions relating to multiple sequence alignments
and operations between them

functions.name: brawn.alignment.get_indices_by_decreasing_value
documentation:
Returns a list of indices, sorted by decreasing value in the
given list

functions.name: brawn.alignment.tree_from_alignment
documentation:
Builds a tree of sequences from the given alignment

Arguments:
    alignment: the alignment to build a tree from

Returns:
    the resulting Tree

functions.name: brawn.alignment.build_query_result
documentation:
Aligns a query sequence to match the alignment path

Arguments:
    sequence: the sequence to align
    path: the path of the alignment

Returns:
    the aligned sequence as a string

functions.name: brawn.alignment.build_reference_result
documentation:
Aligns a reference sequence to match the alignment path

Arguments:
    sequence: the sequence to align
    path: the path of the alignment

Returns:
    the aligned sequence as a string

functions.name: brawn.alignment.combine_alignments
documentation:
Aligns the sequences in the query and reference alignments,
keeping individual columns of each alignment unchanged.

Arguments:
    query: the query alignment
    reference: the reference alignment

Returns:
    the combined alignment

functions.name: brawn.alignment.insert_into_alignment
documentation:
Inserts the given query sequence into an existing alignment.

Arguments:
    query_sequence: the sequence to insert
    alignment: the alignment to insert the query into

Returns:
    a tuple of
        the aligned query sequence
        a dictionary mapping reference name to reference sequence OR single sequence

functions.name: brawn.alignment.get_aligned_pair
documentation:
Inserts the given query sequence into an existing alignment and calculates
the aligned sequences of the query and the named reference

Arguments:
    query_sequence: the sequence to insert
    reference_name: the name of the reference to return the newly aligned sequence of
    alignment: the alignment to insert the query into

Returns:
    a tuple of
        the aligned query sequence
        the aligned reference sequence of the named reference

documentation: Used when a cache file load is attempted from a mismatching version

classes.name: brawn.alignment.InvalidCacheFormatError
documentation: Used when a cache file load is attempted from a file with an invalid format

A wrapper for sequences to handle conversion to/from gap characters

classes.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.alphabet
documentation: The alphabet used in the alignment

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.column_count
documentation:
The number of columns (i.e. the length of a sequence) in
the alignment

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.names
documentation: The names of the sequences in the alignment

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.sequences
documentation: The sequences of the alignment

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.get_sequence_by_name
documentation: Returns the sequence in the alignment that matches the given name

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.positions
documentation: Returns the positional data for each position in the alignment

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.get_weights
documentation: Gets a list of sequence weights matching the sequence order

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.get_sequence_weight
documentation: Returns the tree weight of the sequence with the given index

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.get_percentage_identity_pair
documentation:
Returns the percentage of similar characters in the two sequences,
skipping any pair which include a gap

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.from_file
documentation:
Constructs an alignment instance from a file handle with
content in FASTA format

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.from_cache_file
documentation: Rebuilds an alignment instance from a cache file

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.to_cache_file
documentation: Saves a cached version of alignment to the given file

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.get_column_ungapped_weight
documentation:
Returns the total sequence weight of all sequences which have a gap in the
given column.

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.get_fractional_weighted_counts
documentation:
Returns the residue weight totals of the given column,
summing for each residue the sequence weight of the sequence
contributing each residue

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.get_gap_open_weight_total
documentation:
Returns the total sequence weight of all sequences which have a gap opening in
the given column.

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.get_gap_close_weight_total
documentation:
Returns the total sequence weight of all sequences which have a gap closing in
the given column.

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.to_file
documentation:
Writes the alignment to file in FASTA format

Arguments:
    columns: the maximum number of columns per line
    handle: the file to write to if not stdout

methods.name: brawn.alignment.Alignment.to_dict
documentation:
Returns a dictionary mapping sequence name to sequence for
each sequence in the alignment

Represents a multiple sequence alignment.

Arguments:
    sequence_by_name: a mapping of entry name to entry sequence
    alphabet: the alphabet of the sequences

classes.name: brawn.alignment.ResultAlignment

methods.name: brawn.alignment.ResultAlignment.column_count
documentation: The number of columns in the resulting alignment

methods.name: brawn.alignment.ResultAlignment.names
documentation: The names of the inputs, starting with those from the query

methods.name: brawn.alignment.ResultAlignment.sequences
documentation: The newly aligned sequences, starting with those from the query

methods.name: brawn.alignment.ResultAlignment.get_aligned_references
documentation: Returns a dictionary of the newly (re-)aligned reference sequences

methods.name: brawn.alignment.ResultAlignment.to_file
documentation:
Writes a FASTA file of the result

Arguments:
    handle: the file handle to write to (defaults to stdout)
    columns: the maximum columns per line for sequences

Returns:
    None

methods.name: brawn.alignment.ResultAlignment.to_dict
documentation: Returns a dictionary mapping name to sequence for each of the inputs

The result of combining two existing alignments via a path.
Members are generally caculated in a lazy fashion, on demand.
